name: Cyera
description: Cyera built its position on fast, agentless discovery and classification across cloud, SaaS, and on-premises connectors, with a recent push into AI pipeline coverage through an AI Guardian module that scans training datasets, vector databases, and inference logs. The platform's detection-and-response capabilities are less developed than its discovery and classification core, so it is frequently paired with a SIEM or DLP for enforcement. Its value depends heavily on the customer's data governance maturity: findings are only as useful as the organization's ability to drive remediation through data owners and platform teams.
best_fit: Large enterprises with complex, hybrid data environments that want fast agentless deployment and are prepared to pair the platform with existing SIEM or DLP infrastructure for enforcement.
strengths: Agentless deployment with full visibility reported within 24-72 hours
AI Guardian module extends classification into training data and vector stores
Strong customer support ratings on enterprise review platforms
watch: Enterprise-level pricing excludes mid-market consideration. Detection-and-response depth lags the discovery and classification core, meaning it is rarely a standalone solution.

name: Sentra
description: Sentra is a newer cloud-only DSPM specialist built around fast time-to-value: agentless deployment, out-of-the-box classifiers, and a clean interface aimed at getting useful findings within days rather than months. It positions itself explicitly around AI-era data exposure, extending classification and posture management to data touched by Copilot, Bedrock, and similar AI surfaces at large scale. The tradeoff for its newness is a smaller third-party integration ecosystem and fewer enterprise reference deployments than longer-established competitors.
best_fit: Cloud-first organizations, particularly mid-market, that want DSPM running in days and need strong AI-pipeline coverage without the deployment overhead of legacy or on-premises-heavy platforms.
strengths: Fast deployment with insights reported on day one
Strong reported classification precision with low false-positive rates
Native coverage of AI and Copilot data surfaces at scale
watch: Fewer enterprise references than incumbents like Cyera or BigID. Smaller integration ecosystem may require additional tooling for complex environments.

name: Symmetry Systems
description: Symmetry Systems built its DataGuard platform around identity-data correlation rather than data discovery alone, fusing the data estate with the full identity graph, including human, service account, AI agent, and third-party vendor identities, so findings show not just what is exposed but exactly who can reach it. The platform can run fully isolated with no external network dependency, which opens it to defense, federal, and high-security healthcare environments that cloud-dependent competitors cannot serve. DataEnforce closes the loop with automated remediation rather than stopping at findings.
best_fit: Federal agencies, defense contractors under DFARS requirements, and high-security healthcare organizations that need identity-aware data risk and air-gapped deployment options.
strengths: Identity-data correlation treats AI agents as first-class identity principals
Can run fully isolated with no external network dependency
DataEnforce provides native automated remediation without a separate DLP or PAM tool
watch: Premium positioning and deployment depth are built for high-security environments; organizations without that requirement may find lighter cloud-native tools sufficient.

name: Concentric AI
description: Concentric AI's Semantic Intelligence platform uses deep learning rather than upfront rules to classify structured and unstructured content, with particular strength in nuanced unstructured data such as intellectual property, contracts, and collaboration documents. Risk Distance analysis assesses exposure without requiring end-user labeling. The platform extends into on-premises file shares and Microsoft Copilot data governance, and includes autonomous remediation of risky permissions and sharing rather than stopping at alerting.
best_fit: Organizations whose primary exposure is in unstructured content — intellectual property, contracts, business-confidential documents — across both cloud and on-premises file shares.
strengths: Deep learning classification reduces reliance on regex rules and manual labeling
Strong on-premises coverage including Windows file shares and legacy repositories
Visual data lineage included in base licensing rather than as a paid add-on
watch: Smaller company profile than category leaders; evaluate reference depth for large, complex multi-cloud estates.

name: Wiz (Google Cloud)
description: Wiz's DSPM module scans the actual content of cloud storage and databases to detect PII, PCI, and PHI, correlating findings with misconfiguration and identity data inside its broader Security Graph so teams see full attack-path context rather than isolated data findings. Google completed its acquisition of Wiz for approximately $32 billion in March 2026, the largest cybersecurity acquisition in history, with Wiz continuing to operate with stated independence under Google Cloud. The agentless, snapshot-based architecture avoids cross-cloud data transfer regardless of which provider hosts the source workload.
best_fit: Organizations already running or evaluating Wiz for broader cloud security that want data sensitivity findings correlated directly with infrastructure misconfigurations and identity risk in one platform.
strengths: Data content scanning (not just permission checks) across major cloud storage and database services
Native correlation between data sensitivity and attack-path context in the Security Graph
AI-SPM module discovers shadow AI models, training datasets, and unrestricted inference endpoints
watch: Acquired by Google for $32B, closing March 2026. Buyers should confirm multi-cloud parity commitments in writing, since Google has structural incentive to favor GCP over AWS and Azure coverage over time, and should review the integration roadmap with Google's existing security portfolio.

name: Palo Alto Networks Prisma Cloud
description: Prisma Cloud's DSPM module uses out-of-band analysis, scanning data copies or snapshots rather than connecting directly to production data, which avoids performance impact on live systems and eliminates data-transfer costs when scanning happens inside the customer's own cloud account. DSPM sits alongside CSPM, CWPP, CIEM, and AI-SPM modules within the broader credit-based Prisma Cloud licensing model. As with other CNAPP-embedded DSPM, coverage of on-premises data repositories is limited relative to dedicated DSPM specialists.
best_fit: Enterprises already standardized on Prisma Cloud for cloud security posture management that want data sensitivity findings correlated with existing misconfiguration and identity risk data.
strengths: Out-of-band scanning avoids production performance impact and data-transfer costs
Tight correlation with existing CSPM, CWPP, and CIEM findings inside one platform
Credit-based licensing allows incremental adoption alongside existing Prisma Cloud modules
watch: Credit-based pricing can scale unpredictably with ephemeral, dynamic cloud workloads. DSPM depth and on-premises coverage trail dedicated DSPM specialists; evaluate accordingly if hybrid coverage is a requirement.

name: Orca Security
description: Orca built its CNAPP on proprietary SideScanning technology, which analyzes cloud infrastructure from outside the runtime environment rather than deploying agents, reaching full visibility across AWS, Azure, and GCP within roughly 24 hours of connecting an account. DSPM capabilities, added in 2023, identify sensitive data at risk across managed and self-hosted cloud data stores as well as files on virtual machines, containers, and storage buckets, combining data intelligence with the platform's broader vulnerability and malware risk context rather than treating data discovery as a standalone feed.
best_fit: Lean security and DevOps teams running multi-cloud environments who want agentless visibility across vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, and data risk consolidated in a single platform without deploying agents.
strengths: SideScanning architecture delivers full visibility within roughly 24 hours of connection
DSPM findings are correlated with the platform's broader attack-path analysis, not siloed
No published per-workload list pricing means costs scale with negotiated contract terms rather than rigid tiers
watch: Custom enterprise-only pricing with no public tiers makes upfront cost comparison difficult. On-premises coverage is limited, consistent with its cloud-native CNAPP architecture.

name: CrowdStrike Falcon Data Security
description: CrowdStrike's data security capability, built on the 2024 Flow Security acquisition and relaunched in 2026 as Falcon Data Security, extends beyond data at rest into runtime visibility, monitoring sensitive data in motion across APIs, cloud services, and applications. Its differentiator inside the Falcon ecosystem is correlation with endpoint and identity telemetry: a data exposure finding can be cross-referenced against endpoint sensor coverage, associated identity credentials, and observed threat activity in one console, closing the gap legacy DSPM tools leave between data at rest and data in motion.
best_fit: Organizations running CrowdStrike Falcon for endpoint and identity that want data discovery, runtime data-flow monitoring, and threat correlation unified in one platform without adding a separate vendor.
strengths: Runtime visibility into data in motion, not just data at rest
Native correlation between data exposure and existing Falcon endpoint and identity telemetry
Agentless cloud scanning combined with endpoint-native detection
watch: Value proposition is strongest for organizations already standardized on Falcon; outside that ecosystem it is a newer, less proven data security entrant relative to dedicated DSPM specialists.

name: BigID
description: BigID is the broadest-coverage DSPM platform in the market, deployable across IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, code repositories, big data and NoSQL pipelines, and on-premises systems, with a classification taxonomy built on more than 1,500 classifiers covering global privacy regulations. The platform bridges DSPM and privacy governance more deeply than cloud-native competitors, with mature workflows for DSAR automation, consent management, and data subject rights that make it a default consideration for compliance-heavy sectors. The breadth comes with deployment complexity: enterprise rollouts typically take months rather than weeks to reach steady state.
best_fit: Regulated enterprises with hybrid, multi-source data estates and significant privacy-program requirements where classification taxonomy depth and compliance workflow maturity outweigh deployment speed.
strengths: Broadest connector and environment coverage in the category, including mainframes and NoSQL pipelines
1,500+ classifiers with deep privacy regulation mapping (GDPR, CCPA, and others)
Mature DSAR, consent, and data subject rights workflows beyond core DSPM
watch: Deployment complexity and timeline exceed lighter cloud-native competitors. Pricing is reported as higher than alternatives, which can be a barrier for cloud-only organizations wanting fast time-to-value.

name: Varonis
description: Varonis built the deepest behavioral analytics in the category by instrumenting data access directly rather than relying on point-in-time scanning, producing findings cloud-native competitors cannot match, such as flagging a service account accessing data stores it had never touched before at an unusual hour. Its heritage is on-premises file shares, Active Directory, SharePoint, Exchange, and Microsoft 365, with cloud coverage (AWS, Azure, GCP) expanded substantially through 2024-2026 though still considered less differentiated than purpose-built cloud-native classification engines.
best_fit: Organizations whose primary data security exposure is in on-premises file shares, Active Directory, and Microsoft 365, who value behavioral threat detection over lightweight cloud-native scanning.
strengths: Deepest behavioral analytics and activity audit trail in the category
Unmatched depth on Microsoft 365, Active Directory, and traditional file shares
Forensic audit trails support regulatory access-control and breach-notification requirements directly
watch: Varonis is ending support for self-hosted on-premises deployments on December 31, 2026. Existing on-prem customers must migrate to Varonis SaaS, re-scan all Microsoft 365 and on-premises data, and budget for higher associated costs. Cloud-native classification accuracy and deployment simplicity trail specialists like Cyera and Sentra for cloud-only estates.

name: Netwrix (1Secure)
description: Netwrix 1Secure unifies DSPM, data loss prevention, identity threat detection and response, and privileged access management under one platform, built explicitly around the position that data risk cannot be understood separately from identity risk. Coverage spans Active Directory, Entra ID, file servers, NAS devices, Microsoft 365, databases, and cloud storage, with deployment measured in weeks rather than the multi-month implementation timelines associated with larger incumbents. The platform is frequently positioned as a Varonis on-premises replacement given its hybrid-first design and confirmed on-premises support beyond 2026.
best_fit: Mid-market and enterprise organizations, particularly Microsoft-heavy hybrid environments, that need data posture and identity risk connected in a single platform without a multi-month deployment.
strengths: Confirmed on-premises support beyond 2026, unlike Varonis's sunsetting on-prem product
Connects DSPM with identity, privilege, and access governance under one platform rather than requiring manual correlation
Faster deployment timeline (weeks) relative to enterprise incumbents
watch: Smaller market presence and integration ecosystem than category leaders. Best evaluated for hybrid Microsoft-centric estates rather than complex multi-cloud-native environments.

name: IBM Guardium DSPM
description: IBM Guardium DSPM runs exclusively as SaaS, with an analyzer component deployed inside the customer's own cloud account, and does not require any other Guardium product to operate independently. It covers AWS, GCP, and Azure with continuous scanning for shadow data, publicly exposed sensitive data, and exposed secrets, plus a ransomware vulnerability detection engine built on data flow and access intelligence. Pricing is based on the number of database servers covered rather than data volume or workload count.
best_fit: Organizations already in the IBM security ecosystem, or those wanting cloud-only DSPM with secrets detection and ransomware-vulnerability scanning, without committing to the broader Guardium suite.
strengths: No dependency on other Guardium products to operate
Combines DSPM with exposed-secrets detection and ransomware vulnerability scanning
Per-database-server pricing model, distinct from per-workload or per-GB consumption pricing
watch: SaaS-only deployment means no on-premises option, which is a gap for organizations with legacy or air-gapped infrastructure requirements.

name: Microsoft Purview DSPM
description: Microsoft Purview DSPM is the native data security layer for Microsoft 365 and Azure, with its fastest-growing capability being DSPM for AI: general availability in May 2026 extended coverage to monitoring AI agent activity, Copilot interactions, and Microsoft Foundry and Fabric workloads alongside traditional data discovery. Pricing combines per-user-per-month licensing with newer pay-as-you-go meters tied to data flow through networks, GenAI apps, pipelines, and AI agents, reflecting a shift toward billing based on actual data activity rather than seat count alone.
best_fit: Enterprises standardized on Microsoft 365, Azure, and Copilot that need native data security and AI governance integrated directly into the Microsoft ecosystem without a third-party connector layer.
strengths: Deepest native integration with Microsoft 365, Copilot, and Azure of any platform in the category
DSPM for AI extends governance to AI agents and Copilot interactions natively, ahead of most third-party tools
Consumption-based pricing aligns cost with actual data and AI activity rather than flat licensing
watch: Coverage outside the Microsoft ecosystem is materially weaker; third-party SaaS and non-Microsoft cloud coverage typically requires pairing with another DSPM vendor for full visibility.

name: Netskope One DSPM
description: Netskope's DSPM capability is built on its 2024 acquisition of Dasera and positioned as a component of the broader Netskope One SASE and Security Service Edge platform rather than a standalone product. Coverage spans structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data across web, SaaS, PaaS, IaaS, email, endpoint, and on-premises use cases, with the architectural thesis that DSPM is a natural extension of SSE policy enforcement rather than a separate discipline. DSPM is sold as an add-on module within Netskope One editions rather than included in the base SSE bundle.
best_fit: Organizations already running Netskope for SASE or SSE that want data security posture folded into existing network and access policy enforcement rather than adding a standalone DSPM vendor.
strengths: Genuine convergence of data posture with existing SSE policy enforcement, not a bolted-on feature
Coverage extends to PaaS repositories such as Databricks and Snowflake alongside hyperscaler storage
Single console for DSPM and data loss prevention as integration matures
watch: DSPM is an add-on within specific platform editions, not included by default; total cost depends heavily on which Netskope One tier an organization is already licensed for. Integration between Dasera's original technology and the native Netskope console is still maturing.

name: Proofpoint DSPM
description: Proofpoint DSPM, built on the 2024 Normalyze acquisition, takes a human-centric risk framing distinct from most of the category: rather than prioritizing findings purely by data sensitivity, it weighs human behavior such as unauthorized access and over-permissioned accounts. Coverage spans SaaS, PaaS, multi-cloud, on-premises, and hybrid environments, with particular positioning around Microsoft 365 and Copilot data exposure as a faster, lower-cost alternative to legacy on-premises-heavy platforms.
best_fit: Organizations already using Proofpoint for email security or insider threat management that want data security findings prioritized by human behavioral risk, particularly around Microsoft 365 and Copilot exposure.
strengths: Human-centric risk prioritization distinguishes findings by behavioral context, not sensitivity alone
In-place scanning architecture designed for rapid time-to-value relative to legacy platforms
Positioned directly as a faster, lower-cost migration path for organizations leaving Varonis on-premises
watch: Standalone Normalyze pricing is no longer available post-acquisition; DSPM is sold as part of enterprise platform agreements, which can add cost for organizations not otherwise using Proofpoint products.

name: Rubrik DSPM
description: Rubrik's DSPM is built on its 2023 acquisition of Laminar, integrated into the broader Rubrik Security Cloud platform alongside backup, recovery, and ransomware resilience capabilities. The differentiated value is treating backup data and live production data within a single data security view, allowing data classification to inform backup prioritization and recovery sequencing by business criticality rather than running data security and data protection as separate disciplines. The standalone Laminar product no longer exists as an independent purchase; new buyers are evaluating Rubrik DSPM as part of the broader platform.
best_fit: Organizations consolidating data security and backup strategy, particularly where ransomware preparedness and recovery sequencing by data sensitivity is a primary driver, and that are already evaluating Rubrik for backup.
strengths: Unifies data security posture with backup and recovery prioritization in one platform
Classification informs which data gets prioritized backup and recovery sequencing
Cyber-resilience-led architecture provides genuine consolidation value for ransomware-focused buyers
watch: Premium pricing relative to backup-focused alternatives. Standalone DSPM depth does not differentiate against cloud-native specialists for organizations not already committed to Rubrik for backup.

name: Cohesity DSPM (powered by Cyera)
description: Cohesity's DSPM, launched in March 2026, is powered by Cyera's classification engine rather than built natively, closing the loop from data discovery and classification to backup-based protection and recovery. Cohesity completed its merger with Veritas in late 2024, combining Veritas's broad workload coverage heritage with Cohesity's hyperconverged backup architecture and DataHawk threat defense capabilities, positioning the combined entity as a data security and resilience platform rather than a pure DSPM vendor.
best_fit: Enterprises already running or evaluating Cohesity for backup and data resilience that want data classification feeding directly into recovery prioritization, particularly for AI-related data risk.
strengths: Cyera classification engine brings proven discovery and classification accuracy into a backup-native platform
Post-Veritas-merger workload coverage breadth is among the widest of any backup-integrated vendor
Explicit focus on AI-related data risk reduction within the backup and recovery workflow
watch: DSPM capability is recently launched (March 2026) and powered by a third-party engine rather than built natively; evaluate integration maturity directly. Best understood as a resilience platform with DSPM attached, not a standalone DSPM purchase.

name: Securiti (Veeam)
description: Securiti's Data Command Center unifies DSPM with privacy operations automation, data access governance, AI security controls, and compliance management on a proprietary knowledge graph spanning hybrid multicloud and SaaS environments. Veeam acquired Securiti for $1.725 billion in December 2025, positioning the combination as a unified backup-plus-security-plus-privacy data command center; Securiti continues to operate as a separate division within Veeam rather than being immediately absorbed into Veeam's core product line.
best_fit: Large enterprises with significant privacy compliance requirements across 100+ global regulations that want DSPM unified with privacy operations automation, particularly organizations also evaluating Veeam for backup.
strengths: Knowledge-graph architecture unifies DSPM, privacy operations, and AI governance in one platform
Broad global privacy regulation coverage (GDPR, CCPA/CPRA, LGPD, PIPL, and others)
Recognized as a Leader in GigaOm's DSPM evaluation prior to the Veeam acquisition
watch: Acquired by Veeam in December 2025; long-term product direction and pricing structure as it integrates with Veeam's backup-led portfolio is still settling. Evaluate roadmap commitments directly given the recent ownership change.

name: Cyberhaven
description: Cyberhaven's architecture is built on continuous data lineage tracing from origin through every copy, paste, transformation, and upload, across endpoints, cloud, SaaS, and on-premises environments, rather than the periodic scan-based visibility most DSPM platforms rely on. Its February 2026 general availability release unified DSPM with data loss prevention, insider risk management, and AI security in one architecture, with native real-time inline blocking at the endpoint rather than depending on third-party tools for enforcement. The endpoint-native approach addresses a structural gap most cloud-API-based DSPM tools have: limited or no visibility once data leaves a cloud data store.
best_fit: Organizations whose primary data risk is exfiltration and insider threat across endpoints, SaaS, and AI tools, who need real-time enforcement rather than periodic, scan-based posture reporting.
strengths: Continuous, real-time data lineage rather than point-in-time scanning, eliminating blind spots between scan intervals
Native real-time inline blocking at the endpoint without third-party DLP dependency
Unified DSPM, DLP, insider risk, and AI security in a single architecture
watch: Agent-based endpoint architecture is a heavier deployment model than agentless cloud-API competitors; evaluate operational overhead for large, distributed endpoint fleets.

name: DataStealth
description: DataStealth deploys entirely at the network layer via a DNS change, acting as an inline proxy that discovers, classifies, and actively protects data through tokenization, masking, and encryption without requiring agents, code changes, or application modifications. This architecture is the only one in the category that protects mainframe and other legacy systems without code changes, and it positions itself as a full Data Security Platform rather than DSPM alone: visibility plus active enforcement (vaulted, quantum-resistant tokenization) in a single deployment.
best_fit: Organizations with mainframes, legacy applications, or strict data residency requirements where agent- or API-based DSPM tools cannot reach, and that want active data protection (not just visibility) without application changes.
strengths: Only major platform in the category offering true no-code, no-agent protection of mainframe and legacy systems
Combines DSPM-style visibility with active enforcement (tokenization, masking) rather than alerting alone
Deployment via DNS change reaches meaningful visibility in hours rather than weeks
watch: Network-layer architecture is a fundamentally different model from cloud-API DSPM; AI pipeline coverage and cloud-native classification depth are less mature than cloud-first specialists.
